title: Souls or Spirits
wikitext:
[]: "Souls" or "Spirits"?             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Mon Nov 30, 2009 6:37 pm An issue which has been bothering me of late is the generally accepted translation of the Japenese word (魂) "tamashii". Typically, and I believe always in Evangelion, this word is translated into English as "soul". However, "tamashii" apparently also translates as "spirit" . You may well ask; "What's the difference?" and indeed "Who cares?". And while this issue is indeed a minor or even trivial issue, I think it merits some discussion. Not because of the original word, but because of the difference between the meanings of these two translations. I don't think this has been discussed previously. Firstly, we should note that the word "soul" in Graeco-Judeo-Christian cultures has a very definite suite of meanings. The soul is usually something immortal and eternal, and unique to the individual. Something which is even said to define what a person is. One important point is that animals do not have souls in most doctrines. I'm not clear on all the various teachings here, but hopefully others can provide more information. Now, the word "spirit" does not really convey the same meaning in our culture as "soul". It's a slightly "weaker" term when referring to the transcendental. Now, of note on this point is that religion in Japan is largely based on Shinto, which has large aspects of Animism ; that is the idea everything has a soul or spirit*. So for example, people, animals, trees and even rocks or mountains can have souls or spirits. I think you'll agree that spirit is a far less loaded term to use than soul in this second concept. (Again, If anyone knows more about religion in Japan or Animism please contribute) Which brings me to my main question. Is it more appropriate to use "sprit" as a translation of "tamashii" rather than "soul"? The word soul is in some sense a loaded term in most western cultures and carries with it a lot of, perhaps misleading, cultural interpretation. The primary confusion I feel may be caused by the choice of "soul" is its use in things like Theory of Soul Divisibility . "Soul Divisibility" can help a lot in explaining certain things in Evangelion. In particular, how Rei is Lilith, Rei II, Rei I in Unit-01, and Rei III just about anywhere. Also, the case of Kyoko, her contact experiment and subsequent madness is also explained using the idea of a "split soul". In addition to all this, we also have the concept of "soul salvage" and transferral of souls from dead bodies. These points have always lead to confusion, denials and/or rebuttals from many quarters. I confess to having issues with them myself at times. But I think the problem is not the ideas themselves but rather their presentation. By calling the "tamashii" in Evangelion "souls", we inevitably imbue them with quite a lot of cultural baggage which may not be appropriate to the show. "Souls" in the general western understanding by definition can't be split, or salvaged or transferred or anything like what our analysis needs them to do. They are something entirely immaterial. I think that using the word "soul" may be introducing the kind of misconceptions and false impressions that the many religious references in Eva have lead to. Spirit on the other hand is I feel a less loaded term; far more general in its meanings and interpretations. Speaking of Rei's "Spirit" being spilt between at least four beings does not step on quite as many mental or cultural toes. Kyoko's "spirit" being half absorbed into Unit-02 is also far less controversial. "Spirit" salvage and transferral does not seem so unorthodox if considered in the context of animism. To summarise, my question is whether we might be better off using "spirit" in place of "soul" in Evangelion? Would it, like focusing less on the meaning of religious symbols, help focus more on productive analysis of the show? Would it make any difference at all? Is there no point? What do you think? *By the way, here's a short paper I found in my travels which discusses Animism in Japanese animation.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Mon Nov 30, 2009 7:43 pm I think that the problem with using spirit in place of soul is that it is less accurate. The way that it is being used in Eva makes it seem as if the show's creators had the western definition of soul in mind. To the western way of thinking, the soul is the spiritual component of a human. Western thought does, in fact, recognize other sorts of spirits other than human souls, just not in rocks trees and mountains. God, Satan, Angels, and demons are said to be spirits. In this sense of the word they are greater than human souls, not lesser. In other words, all souls are spirits, but not all spirits are souls. Souls would be a subset of spirits. I think soul is probably the proper translation.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Dec 01, 2009 2:32 am I don't see anything to be gained by going with the "spirit" interpretation of the word; especially since "soul" is the official translation, and animism doesn't really play any sort of role in Evangelion whatsoever.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Dec 01, 2009 7:22 am What Wayne and Aura said. Besides, isn't the word "spirit" interchangeable with "soul" in English ? It's pretty evident in phrases such as "To give up the spirit" (die). In addition, there is no other word in Japanese that seems to come close to Tamashii in meaning "soul", however the rather broad "spirit" is matched by an entire gaggle of Japanese translations comprising of different Kanji, including the words Rei, Reikon, Seikon and Seishin, among others. I can't tell for sure what those other "spirits" specifically connote, but it does mean that Tamashii has been translated more than appropriately enough as "soul" and any change to "spirit" would make for severe, self-inflicted confusion. Edit: This explanation on the soul in Judaism might be relevant to NGE. http://www.chabad.org/library/article_cdo/aid/3194/jewish/What-is-a-Soul.htm

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Tue Dec 01, 2009 7:44 am The kanji for tamashii (魂) contains the kanji for oni (鬼) which, aside from those demons refers to a dead person's ghost or spirit. Take from that what you will. I'm not gonna worry with semantics though. I don't think it's that big of a deal. Also, anyone who thinks there aren't animistic themes in Eva either doesn't know much about Shinto or isn't paying attention.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Dec 01, 2009 9:21 am thewayneiac wrote: In other words, all souls are spirits, but not all spirits are souls. Souls would be a subset of spirits.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Tue Dec 01, 2009 10:46 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: thewayneiac wrote: In other words, all souls are spirits, but not all spirits are souls. Souls would be a subset of spirits.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Dec 01, 2009 11:34 am thewayneiac wrote: I understand that the Japanese look at spiritualism differently than westerners, but as presented in NGE how are the human souls more like Japanese spirits than western souls? Give some in-show examples of how these are amimistic spirits and not human souls.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Dec 01, 2009 11:46 am which would certainly seem to support the view animistic themes are present in Evangelion.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Dec 01, 2009 3:00 pm There's a lot more to the subject than the musings of the church fathers and their heirs. There's the old Egyptian multiplicity of souls , for example.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Wed Dec 02, 2009 9:56 am And also, the big discussion about "souls" I had in Eva was not because of the word tamashii but the word kokoro. All because of that ATF is the light of the "kokoro". Anyways, as for the "soul" issue. I always thought Eva with its ATF, soul and body form seemed to take a lot from Aristotle's theory (was it just the theory of knowledge? or more...). I'm a little rusty on that issue right now, but if you're interested I might take a look at how it was.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Dec 02, 2009 5:15 pm I'm interested in it all(though I'm not sure if the old Egyptian view of souls is applicable here). Anything could be relevant. In particular, the use of the word "kokoro" in relation to the AT Field is particularly intriguing.

[]: Re: "Souls" or "Spirits"?             Quote Post by VoidEater » Wed Dec 02, 2009 6:09 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Firstly, we should note that the word "soul" in Graeco-Judeo-Christian cultures has a very definite suite of meanings.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Dec 02, 2009 6:55 pm I think spirit has a connotation for being less encompassing than a soul. It never seemed to have a connotation for holding a person's emotions for me and was just more like a basic essence of life. I think soul goes better with the superficial Christian themes of Eva as well.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Dec 02, 2009 8:03 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I'm interested in it all(though I'm not sure if the old Egyptian view of souls is applicable here).

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Jan 21, 2010 1:04 pm This seems like an issue of semantics and Western audiences' prejudices coloring their interpretations more than one of translation. "Soul" is perfectly fine, IMO. As already mentioned, there are a multiplicity of other Japanese terms to convey to various versions of "spirit", but tamashii is the only one that really translates into "soul" specifically. NGE reinterpreting Judeo-Christian "motifs" is also absolutely nothing new. Folks just need to understand this, and get over it. It should also be noted that something in NGE that is superficially a J-C reference could be heavily imbued with Eastern spiritual ideas. They're not mutually exclusive. Now we just need a matching thread where someone argues that kami should be translated as "deity" or something because there isn't anything in NGE resembling the Judeo-Christian God. EDIT: Another example would be suggesting that we call the Angels "Messengers" instead because some people misconstrue the Angels' nature on account of the name's religious connotations.
